title: Allrecipes Data Final Project
text:
##install.packages("tidytuesdayR")
library(tidytuesdayR)
library(tidyverse)

tuesdata <- tidytuesdayR::tt_load('2025-09-16')

all_recipes <- tuesdata$all_recipes
cuisines <- tuesdata$cuisines

A brief description of your dataset including its provenance, dimensions, etc. as well as the reason why you chose this dataset.
Make sure to load the data and use inline code for some of this information.
The project uses two datasets: all_recipes and cuisines. The all_recipes dataset has 14,426 rows and 16 columns, representing 14,426 recipes. The columns are name, url, author, date_published, ingredients, calories, fat, carbs, protein, avg_rating, total_ratings, reviews, prep_time, cook_time, total_time, and servings. The cuisines dataset has 2,218 rows and 17 columns represting 2,218 recipes categorized by the country they originated from. . The columns are name, country, url, author, date published, ingredients, calories, fat, carbs, protein, avg_rating, total_ratings, reviews, prep_time, cook_time, total_time, and servings. I chose these datasets because they contain a large amount of recipes and have information on both recipe popularity and nutritional content. This opens up the project to more types of analysis and potential visualizations!

title: Allrecipes Data Final Project
text:
Questions
The two questions you want to answer.
Question 1: How do the prep time, cook time, and total recipe time affect recipe ratings?
Question 2: How do protein, fat, and carb content vary across different cuisine categories?

title: Allrecipes Data Final Project
text:
Analysis plan

A plan for answering each of the questions including the variables involved, variables to be created (if any), external data to be merged in (if any).

For question 1, I will look at prep time and cook time seperately, as well as the total time to make the recipe. I will make a scatterplot of the rating versus total time. Additionally, I will split the times into groups based on total time (ie. quick recipes under 30 minutes, moderate recipes 30-60 minutes, and long recipes that are over an hour) and make a boxplot of the ratings across the time groups. This will identify whether recipes that require longer or shorter amounts of time receive different ratings from users.
For question 2, I will make boxplots comparing the nutrient distributions across different cuisines to see how the nutrients differ in different types of food. Because the dataset contains many countries, the analysis will focus on the 3 most common cuisine types. Additionally, I will also look at more basic summary statistics like the average amount of nutrients in cuisine types.

title: Project title
text:
Math Expressions
You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. $\alpha+\beta$ renders \(\alpha + \beta\). You can use the display style with double dollar signs:
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
\[
\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i
\]
Limitations:

The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting $$ must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending $$ must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;
There should not be spaces after the opening $ or before the closing $.

title: Project title
text:
Feeling adventurous?

You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that’s what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.
But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.
